summary: This notebook demonstrates known bugs in the official Gemma-3n-E2B vision model checkpoints and provides working inference recipes. It shows that running the model in FP16 on standard GPUs causes activation overflows, while the original checkpoints contain a transposed spatial dimension bug in the MobileNetV5 vision tower that breaks OCR tasks. The author proves that using the Unsloth library resolves both issues automatically, whereas the standard `transformers` pipeline requires manual tensor transposition and FP32/CPU execution as a workaround. Readers should take away the importance of verifying model loader compatibility and being aware of checkpoint-specific spatial or precision bugs when deploying vision-language models.
competition_flow: Loads OCR test images → processes them through Gemma-3n-E2B vision model → generates text predictions while demonstrating and working around FP16 overflow and spatial transposition bugs.
data_reading: Images are loaded from a Kaggle dataset input path using PIL (`Image.open`).
data_processing: For the standard `transformers` pipeline, manually transposes the `pixel_values` tensor axes (`permute(0,1,3,2)`) and forces FP32 precision on CPU to avoid FP16 activation overflows.
insights: Gemma-3n-E2B's raw activations overflow in FP16 on standard hardware like Kaggle T4 GPUs.
The official checkpoints contain a transposed width/height axis bug in the MobileNetV5 vision tower that breaks OCR.
Unsloth's optimized loader automatically resolves both precision and tensor shape bugs.
Standard `transformers` requires manual pixel value transposition and FP32/CPU execution as a reliable workaround.
critical_findings: FP16 inference on CUDA fails completely due to activation overflow.
The official Hugging Face checkpoints have incorrectly transposed spatial dimensions in the vision tower, causing poor OCR performance.
what_did_not_work: Standard `transformers` pipeline with FP16 on CUDA fails entirely due to activation overflow.
Running the official checkpoint without manual tensor transposition produces incorrect OCR results.
